results.title: All orders can be hijacked to lock rental assets forever by tipping a malicious ERC20
results.reasoning: The described issue is Seaport tipping-based: the Zone/Create logic does not bind or validate consideration extensions (tips), allowing a malicious ERC20 to be appended to the consideration array, which later causes settlement/stop to revert and locks assets. None of the audit items mention tipping, consideration extensions, or Zone allocation of extra consideration items. The closest item (#12) concerns fee-on-transfer ERC20 accounting in PaymentEscrow causing settlement failures—different root cause, code path, and fix. Other listed issues (e.g., PAYEE path, hooks, guard checks) are unrelated. Therefore, the specific tipping-based hijack was not detected in the audit content.

results.title: An attacker is able to hijack any ERC721 / ERC1155 he borrows because guard is missing validation on the address supplied to function call setFallbackHandler()
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report’s issue #18 flags that Guard._checkTransaction does not handle the Gnosis Safe setFallbackHandler(address) selector (0xf08a0323), meaning the safe owner can freely set the fallback handler. The described vulnerability exploits this exact omission by setting the fallback handler to the rented token contract and then forwarding transferFrom via the safe, enabling NFT theft. Both issues are the same root cause and code path (missing validation/blocking of setFallbackHandler in Guard). The remediation suggested in the audit (block setFallbackHandler) would also prevent the hijack described, so fixing the report’s issue would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: An attacker can hijack any ERC1155 token he rents due to a design issue in reNFT via reentrancy exploitation
results.reasoning: The described issue is a creation-time ERC1155 reentrancy/hijack: Seaport transfers ERC1155 to the renter’s Safe before the rental is registered, triggering onERC1155Received via the Safe’s fallback handler, which then re-enters and moves the tokens out while the Guard/state is unaware. None of the audit findings identify this order-of-operations + receiver-callback reentrancy during Create/validateOrder/_rentFromZone. The closest item (#18) concerns not blocking setFallbackHandler, but that is a different root cause and fix (blocking a Safe config change) and does not address the fundamental “transfer before registration” design flaw. The proposed fix in the description (intermediary transfer/register-then-transfer) is not present in the report. Therefore, the report did not detect the same vulnerability.

results.title: Malicious actor can steal any actively rented NFT and freeze the rental payments (of the affected rentals) in the escrow contract
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report explicitly identified the same root issues and code paths enabling the described attack. In particular: (1) Finding #5 (Signer._deriveRentalOrderHash omits rentalWallet) matches the core collision/substitution flaw used to reclaim assets from a victim Safe and then delete the wrong order entry, leaving the real rental unstoppable and payments stuck. Its impact section even states NFTs can be reclaimed from the wrong Safe and the original order becomes un-stoppable, aligning with the ‘steal NFT and freeze payments’ outcome. (2) Finding #6 (OrderMetadata hash omits orderType/emittedExtraData) covers the EIP-712 misuse enabling generic/reusable signatures and altered order semantics, which the exploit relies on to create/fulfill orders during the callback. (3) Finding #10 (CEI violation in stopRent: external NFT callback before storage removal) captures the same stopRent callback window used in the exploit flow. These findings reference the same functions (Stop.stopRent/_reclaimRentedItems, Signer._deriveRentalOrderHash, Signer._deriveOrderMetadataHash) and fixes (including rentalWallet in the order hash, correcting EIP-712 metadata hashing) that would remediate the reported exploit. Thus, the audit did detect the same vulnerability mechanism.

results.title: Escrow contract can be drained by creating rentals that bypass execution invariant checks
results.reasoning: The described issue is a Create.sol execution-invariant bypass: by crafting Seaport matches where to == from, totalExecutions becomes zero, so _executionInvariantChecks are skipped; storage and escrow accounting are still updated, allowing stopRent to drain PaymentEscrow despite no transfers. None of the 21 audit findings mention this code path or mechanism (no references to totalExecutions, _executionInvariantChecks, or zero-length executions). The closest items (e.g., #16 PAYEE orders zero on-chain footprint) concern different logic and opposite effects (no storage updates), and would not be fixed by the same remedy. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected in the audit content.

results.title: Attacker can lock lender NFTs and ERC20 in the safe if the offer is set to partial
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit finding #21 describes the exact same issue: multiple partial fills of the same Seaport order produce the same _deriveRentalOrderHash, while Storage tracks only a boolean orders[orderHash]. On stop, removeRentals deletes the single entry, leaving remaining assets locked and subsequent stops reverting with OrderDoesNotExist. It cites the same code paths (Signer._deriveRentalOrderHash, Create building identical RentalOrder, Storage orders boolean) and proposes the same class of fixes (unique nonce/per-fill identifier or track multiplicity). Although the audit emphasizes same-block fills due to startTimestamp = block.timestamp, it’s the same underlying flaw and fix, so addressing it would resolve the described vulnerability.
